body:
Enables the `commentstart` kube-api-linter (KAL) rule for the `imagepolicy` API group by removing it from the rule's exclusion list in `hack/kube-api-linter/exceptions.yaml` and updating the field godoc comments in `imagepolicy/v1alpha1` to start with the serialized (JSON) field name, as the rule requires.

This is part of the wider effort to enable KAL rules on the built-in API groups one group at a time.

**What:**
- Drop `imagepolicy` from the `commentstart` exclusion regex.
- Update the 10 field godoc comments in `staging/src/k8s.io/api/imagepolicy/v1alpha1/types.go` to begin with their JSON field name.
- Regenerate the affected generated files (swagger doc, protobuf `.proto`, OpenAPI, golangci config, client-go applyconfigurations) — kept in a separate commit.

**Verification:**
- `hack/verify-golangci-lint.sh staging/src/k8s.io/api/...` → 0 issues.
- `hack/verify-codegen.sh` → passes.

Part of #134671

/sig api-machinery
/area code-generation


#### What this PR does / why we need it:

In upgrade scenarios:

Static Pods are created while the apiserver is running.
The apiserver may restart during the upgrade process and become unavailable.
When requests to create corresponding mirror pods are sent, they will time out (taking over 30 seconds) due to the apiserver being inaccessible, resulting in significant latency.
If multiple static Pods exist, creating their mirror pods can consume a substantial amount of time in total.
This will negatively impact the overall Pod creation latency.

### What happened?

In upgrade scenarios:
1. Static Pods are created while the apiserver is running.
2. The apiserver may restart during the upgrade process and become unavailable.
3. When requests to create corresponding mirror pods are sent, they will time out (taking over 30 seconds) due to the apiserver being inaccessible, resulting in significant latency.
4. If multiple static Pods exist, creating their mirror pods can consume a substantial amount of time in total.
5. This will negatively impact the overall Pod creation latency.

### What did you expect to happen?

Asynchronously create a mirror pod for the static pod if it does not already exist. Improve pod creation efficiency.

### How can we reproduce it (as minimally and precisely as possible)?

#### What this PR does / why we need it:
This PR introduces a cacheIntervalSource interface with three implementations:
  - circularBufferSource: existing batched circular buffer path (unchanged)
  - storeSource: existing eager store copy (unchanged, used as fallback)
  - snapshotSource: grabs an immutable snapshot reference under RLock in O(1), then iterates it lazily in the processInterval goroutine outside the lock

When ListFromCacheSnapshot is enabled, WatchList lock hold time drops from O(N) to O(1).

running with `benchstat`(compares a baseline version with an optimized one) shows:

```

  WatchList/UseIndex=false (P)  │   16.099m   │  6.974m   -56.68% (p=0.008) │
  WatchList/UseIndex=true  (P)  │   13.248m   │  6.375m   -51.88% (p=0.008) │
  ```

labels.description: Indicates a PR lacks a `priority/foo` label and requires one.

labels.name: needs-triage
labels.description: Indicates an issue or PR lacks a `triage/foo` label and requires one.

body:
#### What type of PR is this?

/kind bug
/sig node

#### What changed

`ActualStateOfWorld` now rejects nil `VolumeSpec` values before storing pod mount state through `AddPodToVolume` or `CheckAndMarkVolumeAsUncertainViaReconstruction`.

#### Why

Both paths store `MarkVolumeOpts.VolumeSpec` in `mountedPod.volumeSpec`. If that spec is nil, later ASW operations can dereference it while checking remounts or reporting volume state. The cache should return an error and leave ASW unchanged instead of preserving invalid pod-volume state.

#### Which issue(s) this PR is related to:

Related to #109717

#### How tested

- `go test ./pkg/kubelet/volumemanager/cache -run 'Test_(AddPodToVolume_Negative_NilVolumeSpec|CheckAndMarkVolumeAsUncertainViaReconstruction_Negative_NilVolumeSpec)$' -count=1`
- `go test ./pkg/kubelet/volumemanager/cache -count=1`
- `gofmt -l pkg/kubelet/volumemanager/cache/actual_state_of_world.go pkg/kubelet/volumemanager/cache/actual_state_of_world_test.go`
- `git diff --check`

`make test WHAT=./pkg/kubelet/volumemanager/cache` was not run successfully in this local macOS environment because Kubernetes scripts require Bash >= 4.2 and `/bin/bash` is 3.2 here.

title: DRA: Duplicated configs reported in resourceclaim status

labels.name: kind/bug
labels.description: Categorizes issue or PR as related to a bug.

labels.name: needs-triage
labels.description: Indicates an issue or PR lacks a `triage/foo` label and requires one.

labels.name: wg/device-management
labels.description: Categorizes an issue or PR as relevant to WG Device Management.

body:
### What happened?

When creating a resourceclaim, the status will reports more config than it should. Each config will be reported the amount of request it is pointing to. 

This also means that, if we create 32 requests (the allowed limit), and create 3 configs that points to all requests (so 32, this is also allowed), the allocator would want to report 96 configs (3*32) in the status, which would be over the allowed limit of 64 status configs.

Does this has to be backported, what do you think?

### What did you expect to happen?

Only 1 config should be reported in the resourceclaim status,
```yaml
apiVersion: resource.k8s.io/v1             
kind: ResourceClaim                        
metadata:
  name: demo
spec:
  devices:
   config: # Only 1 config is added.
    - opaque:
        driver: my-example-driver
        parameters:
          deviceAttribute: deviceAttributeValue
      requests:
      - request1
      - request2
    requests:
    - name: request1
      exactly:
        allocationMode: ExactCount
        count: 1
        deviceClassName: hostnetworkdevice
    - name: request2
```

body:
/kind feature
/kind cleanup

- Adds `StartTestServerProcess` to the integration framework, which allows integration tests to cleanly start the API Server as an independent subprocess rather than an in-process goroutine. This allows us to fully reconfigure, stop, and restart the API server mid-test to mimic real-world lifecycle events.
   - Introduces `TestFeatureRollbackDoesNotChoke`, an integration test that leverages this out-of-process framework to start an API server with a feature gate enabled, create resources that use the feature, and then restart the API server with the feature gate *disabled*. 
   - The test ensures that reading, updating, and deleting those resources behaves correctly when rolled back, preventing regressions. The rollback behaviors of both `EnvFiles` and `H2CContainerProbe` are fully validated here.

```release-note
NONE
```

body:
Enables the `optionalorrequired` and `nonpointerstructs` kube-api-linter rules for the `authorization` API group. Part of #134671, addresses #136861.

**What:**
- Tags every field in `authorization/v1` and `v1beta1` as `+optional` / `+required`.
- `*.Status` fields are server-populated and optional on input, so they stay `+optional` via a scoped `nonpointerstructs` exception (they can't become pointers without breaking these stable APIs). Happy to mark them `+required` instead if that's preferred — `status` may tighten per API conventions.
- Generated protobuf and OpenAPI specs regenerated; no behavior change.

```release-note
Updated the authorization API group to improve OpenAPI schema correctness for fields being optional or required.
```

/sig auth
/assign @JoelSpeed

title: Discussion: do we need an idle or suspend state for warm worker Pods?

labels.name: sig/scheduling
labels.description: Categorizes an issue or PR as relevant to SIG Scheduling.

labels.name: sig/node
labels.description: Categorizes an issue or PR as relevant to SIG Node.

labels.name: needs-triage
labels.description: Indicates an issue or PR lacks a `triage/foo` label and requires one.

body:
### What would you like to discuss?

I want to discuss if Kubernetes needs some standard semantic between "running"
and "deleted" for warm worker Pods.

This is not a concrete proposal to add a new API now. I am also not asking to
expose Docker `stop/start` directly in Kubernetes. I just want to check if this
is a real gap, or if there is already some existing way / ongoing work to solve
this problem.

For some agent or batch workloads, we usually have worker Pods. When the worker
is idle, there are two simple choices today:

1. keep the worker Pod running
2. delete the worker Pod and create it again later

Both choices have problems.

If we keep the worker running, the warm state is kept, but resources are still
reserved. If we delete it, resources are released, but the next task needs to
create everything again and pay cold start cost.

So I wonder if Kubernetes needs a better way to describe this middle state:

```text
the worker is not doing work now,
but we may want to reuse it later,
and maybe we do not want to delete it completely
```

### Why is this useful?

In agent workloads, sandbox workloads, and some batch workloads, a manager may
create or pre-warm some workers. The manager sends tasks to workers. After a
task is finished, the worker may become idle.

Deleting the worker can reclaim resources, but it also loses warm context. For
example, the system may need to pay again for:

- scheduling
- Pod sandbox creation
- image or container startup
- volume setup
- device setup
- framework initialization
- workspace or cache rebuild

For long running agent sessions or sandbox environments, this can be expensive.
But keeping all idle workers running is also not good for resource usage.

So I want to understand if Kubernetes should have a common model for this, or if
this should stay in application/controller logic.

### Example systems

I see similar patterns in some Kubernetes based agent or sandbox systems.

#### HiClaw

HiClaw uses a manager and workers architecture for multi-agent workloads. The
manager coordinates multiple worker agents. A worker may run a task and then
become idle.

Today the controller can delete idle workers to release resources. But if a new
task comes later, the worker needs to be created again. A warm worker state may
help this kind of workload, if the worker can be idle and reused later.

Project:
https://github.com/agentscope-ai/HiClaw

#### agent-sandbox

agent-sandbox provides Sandbox APIs for stateful agent sandboxes. It also has
warm pool style management. A warm pool can keep sandboxes ready, but idle
sandboxes still need a lifecycle policy.

For example, possible policies are:

- keep sandbox running
- delete sandbox and recreate later
- pause / hibernate sandbox
- resize resources down when idle
- checkpoint and restore later

Project:
https://github.com/kubernetes-sigs/agent-sandbox

#### AgentCube

AgentCube is also related to AI agent workloads. It has concepts like stateful
lifecycle, sleep/resume, and high density resource usage.

This is another example where idle does not always mean the workload can be
deleted. The session may want to keep context or workspace, but the cluster may
not want to count it as a fully active worker.

Project:
https://github.com/volcano-sh/agentcube

### Current choices I know

I may miss something, so please correct me if there is already a better way.

#### Keep the Pod running

The controller keeps the Pod alive and marks it idle in application logic.

Pros:

- fastest reuse
- keeps Pod identity, volume, workspace and local state
- works today

Cons:

- resources are still reserved
- process may still use CPU or memory
- scheduler and quota still see the Pod by its requests

#### Delete and recreate the Pod

The controller deletes idle workers and creates new workers later.

Pros:

- simple
- releases resources clearly
- works with current scheduling and quota model

Cons:

- loses warm state
- has cold start cost next time
- needs to rebuild runtime / workspace / framework state

#### In-place Pod resize

Maybe the controller can reduce CPU or memory requests when the worker is idle,
and increase them again when work comes.

Pros:

- can reduce resource footprint
- uses existing Kubernetes direction

Cons:

- it is only resource resize, not an execution lifecycle state
- the workload still needs to stop or idle by itself
- resume may fail or wait if resources cannot be allocated again

#### Pod checkpoint / restore

Pod checkpoint and restore may solve some suspend/resume use cases.

Pros:

- can keep more state
- may release resources

Cons:

- it is heavier than simple warm worker idle
- needs runtime support
- maybe too complex for some warm pool cases

Related:
https://github.com/kubernetes/enhancements/issues/5823

#### Dynamic Containers

Dynamic Containers may allow adding and removing main containers in a running
Pod.

Pros:

- a Pod can be used more like a reusable execution envelope
- worker containers can maybe be added or removed inside this Pod

Cons:

- removing a container means terminating it
- adding it again creates a new container instance
- it is not the same as stop and resume the same worker container

Related:
https://github.com/kubernetes/enhancements/issues/5972

### Scheduling and resource accounting questions

I think the hard part is resource accounting.

If a stopped or idle Pod still stays on the same Node and keeps the same resource
requests, then scheduler behavior is simple. The resources are still reserved.
But this does not really release cluster capacity.

If the idle Pod releases or reduces resources, then there are more questions:

- does the Pod still stay bound to the same Node?
- can other Pods use the released resources?
- is resume guaranteed, or only best effort?
- what happens if the Node has no enough resource when we want to resume?
- can the workload resume on another Node?
- how should ResourceQuota, scheduler cache, Cluster Autoscaler, Kueue and other
  systems count this Pod?
- should this be implemented by resize, checkpoint/restore, Pod condition,
  subresource, or only higher level controller policy?

So maybe "stop" is not one meaning. It may have different levels.

#### Level 1: execution-only idle

The workload stops doing work, but the Pod still keeps all resource requests.

This is simple and fast to reuse, but does not release schedulable resource.

#### Level 2: idle plus resize down

The workload becomes idle and the Pod resources are resized down to a small idle
footprint. When new work comes, resources are resized up again.

This can release some capacity, but resume may not be guaranteed if the Node has
no enough resource.

#### Level 3: checkpoint or hibernate

The workload is checkpointed or hibernated, resources are released, and it is
restored later.

This can release more resources, but needs stronger runtime support and more
complex semantics.

### Questions

I want to ask the community:

- Is this a real gap in Kubernetes?
- Or should this always be solved by higher level controllers?
- Are in-place resize, checkpoint/restore, Dynamic Containers, or application
  level idle logic already enough?
- Is there existing work that I missed?
- Should Kubernetes document a recommended pattern for warm worker pools?
- If Kubernetes ever supports this kind of semantic, should it be Pod-level,
  container-level, or workload-controller-level?
- Should idle workers keep resources, resize down, or release resources?
- If resources are released, should resume be guaranteed or best effort?
- How should scheduler, quota, autoscaler and Kueue count idle workers?
- How should readiness, probes, restartPolicy and Pod phase work with this kind
  of state?

### Related work

- Pod-Level Checkpoint/Restore:
  https://github.com/kubernetes/enhancements/issues/5823

- Dynamic Containers:
  https://github.com/kubernetes/enhancements/issues/5972

- In-place Pod vertical scaling and workload template mutability:
  https://github.com/kubernetes/kubernetes/issues/132436

- Historical discussion around multi-container Pod lifecycle behavior:
  https://github.com/kubernetes/kubernetes/issues/40908

body:
#### What type of PR is this?

/kind bug
/kind regression

#### What this PR does / why we need it:

Since v1.35, `WatchListClient` defaults to enabled. When users construct a `cache.ListWatch` directly, without `ToListWatcherWithWatchListSemantics`, using a fake client, the reflector attempts the WatchList path.

The `ObjectTracker.Watch()` implementation deliver existing objects as `Added` events but never emits the required `k8s.io/initial-events-end` bookmark. Because of that the informer hangs upforever waiting for sync.

My PR makes `ObjectTracker.Watch()` emit the `k8s.io/initial-events-end` bookmark when `SendInitialEvents=true` is set in the watch options which completes the WatchList protocol.

I added a regression test that reproduces the issue using a bare `cache.ListWatch` with a fake metadata client and `WatchListClient` enabled.

#### Which issue(s) this PR is related to:

Fixes #135895

#### Special notes for your reviewer:

* The factory path `NewFilteredMetadataInformer` is not affected because it wraps with `ToListWatcherWithWatchListSemantics` which detects `IsWatchListSemanticsUnsupported()`. The new bookmark code is only reached when users bypass that wrapping.
* The bookmark object type is derived with `reflect.New`. If no objects exist for the GVR then it falls back to `*metav1.PartialObjectMetadata` which works for metadata informers with an empty tracker.
* `IsWatchListSemanticsUnsupported()` is intentionally kept on the tracker and fake client path so the standard factory path continues to opt out of WatchList semantics. This change only makes the tracker implementation correct when WatchList is used directly.

#### Does this PR introduce a user-facing change?

```release-note
Fixed fake client ObjectTracker to emit the initial-events-end bookmark during WatchList, preventing informers from hanging when WatchListClient is enabled and cache.ListWatch is constructed without ToListWatcherWithWatchListSemantics.
```

#### Additional documentation e.g., KEPs (Kubernetes Enhancement Proposals), usage docs, etc.:

```docs
NONE
```

body: #### What type of PR is this? /kind feature /kind api-change #### What this PR does / why we need it: This PR seeks to add support for optional headers to be passed via the `client.authentication` api. There are situations where you may want to host your Kubernetes API behind a Zero Trust proxy. These proxies require custom headers. For example, if you're using Cloudflare Zero Trust Access Proxy then you may want to pass in your Cloudflare Client ID/Secret. Here's how that would look practically: `~/.kube/cf-auth.sh`: ```bash #!/bin/bash export TOKEN="" export CLOUDFLARE_CLIENT_ID="" export CLOUDFLARE_CLIENT_SECRET="" echo '{ "apiVersion": "client.authentication.k8s.io/v1", "kind": "ExecCredential", "status": { "token": "'$TOKEN'", "authProxyHeaders": { "CF-Access-Client-Id": "'"$CLOUDFLARE_CLIENT_ID"'", "CF-Access-Client-Secret": "'"$CLOUDFLARE_CLIENT_SECRET"'" } } }' ``` `~/.kube/config`: ```yaml users: - name: cloudflare-user user: exec: apiVersion: client.authentication.k8s.io/v1 args: - -c - ~/.kube/cf-auth.sh command: /bin/bash ``` Without this change the headers would simply get dropped, leaving you unable to authenticate with the proxy and `kubectl` can't parse the response: However, after introducing the `authProxyHeaders` field, we can call `kubectl` like normal and it will automatically propagate the custom headers: #### Which issue(s) this PR is related to: Fixes # 1352 https://github.com/kubernetes/kubectl/issues/1352 #### Special notes for your reviewer: Recognize this has security implications and totally understand if there is push-back to implementing this. My current workaround is to set my default `kubectl` path to this branches local binary. I noticed a similar, rather more hacky, pattern also seems to be the recommended workaround from latest StackOverflow post on this subject ([link](https://stackoverflow.com/questions/53481053/how-to-add-custom-http-headers-when-using-kubectl-tool)). If this is not the desired approach, do you have any recommendations on how we could go about something like this? I'm a first-time contributor so I'd love to better understand the proper way for configuring `kubectl` to authenticate with k8s api servers protected behind third-party proxies. #### Does this PR introduce a user-facing change? ```release-note added an optional `authProxyHeaders` field to the `client.authentication.k8s.io/v1` `ExecCredentialStatus` type. Exec credential plugins can use this string key/value map to return HTTP headers for authenticating to a proxy or gateway in front of the Kubernetes API server. ``` #### Additional documentation e.g., KEPs (Kubernetes Enhancement Proposals), usage docs, etc.: ```docs ```

<!--  Thanks for sending a pull request!  Here are some tips for you:

1. If this is your first time, please read our contributor guidelines: https://git.k8s.io/community/contributors/guide/first-contribution.md#your-first-contribution and developer guide https://git.k8s.io/community/contributors/devel/development.md#development-guide
2. Please label this pull request according to what type of issue you are addressing, especially if this is a release targeted pull request. For reference on required PR/issue labels, read here:
https://git.k8s.io/community/contributors/devel/sig-release/release.md#issuepr-kind-label
3. Ensure you have added or ran the appropriate tests for your PR: https://git.k8s.io/community/contributors/devel/sig-testing/testing.md
4. If you want *faster* PR reviews, read how: https://git.k8s.io/community/contributors/guide/pull-requests.md#best-practices-for-faster-reviews
5. If the PR is unfinished, see how to mark it: https://git.k8s.io/community/contributors/guide/pull-requests.md#marking-unfinished-pull-requests
-->

#### What type of PR is this?

/kind flake

#### What this PR does / why we need it:

Fixes [Flaking Test] [sig-autoscaling] Horizontal pod autoscaling (scale resource: CPU)

The test asserts replicas stay in [firstScale, firstScale+1] during a 10-min stasis window. `ConsumeCPU` helper POSTs once to the `ResourceConsumer` Service, and kube-proxy LBs it randomly to a single pod. The metrics snapshot can catch two pods at ~50% util and one idle, which will average ~33% against a 20% target, which drives desired = ceil(1.67 × 3) = 5 and breaches the bound.

Fix: add `perPodCPULoad` to`HPAScaleTest`. When set, CPU load goes through the existing `ConsumeCPUPerPod` helper, which splits total/N evenly across pods via pod proxy and bypasses the random LB. Steady state becomes 250m / 3 = 83.3m/pod = 16.7% util, which causes desired to be at 3. Only the explicit cpuBurst can drive `secondScale=5`.

Not sure what exact change made the test flaky, though. Maybe the recent changes to HPA controller responsiveness could be a cause?

The flag is wired from `checkStability` in `scaleUp`/`scaleDown`, so it only activates on the two tests that assert replica stasis. All other callers of `HPAScaleTest.run` are unchanged. This keeps the assertions written as-is.

#### Which issue(s) this PR is related to:

Fixes #139200

#### Special notes for your reviewer:
This PR fixes the test's load injection, not the controller's response to skewed snapshots. I went this way because the test's [3, 4] bound caps the per-snapshot average pod utilization at 26.67%, and `ConsumeCPU`'s random LB produces snapshots well above that whenever it dumps the full load on one pod. The load injection felt like the right layer to address.

```release-note
NONE
```


body:
#### What type of PR is this?

/kind bug
/kind api-change
/sig api-machinery

#### What this PR does / why we need it

Adds a custom `UnmarshalJSON` to `ResourceList` that skips null-valued entries instead of deserializing them as zero-value Quantity. Previously, `kubectl create` with a YAML containing `cpu: null` in resource limits would convert it to `cpu: 0`, causing a validation error or incorrect resource allocation. `kubectl apply` already handled this correctly by stripping nulls during strategic merge patch, so this makes the behavior consistent.

#### Which issue(s) this PR fixes

Fixes #135423

#### Special notes for your reviewer

A previous attempt at this fix was made in #135468 but went stale. The approach is the same (custom UnmarshalJSON on ResourceList) but with a clean commit history and passing CI.

This is an API-level change in the deserialization behavior of `ResourceList`. Null values in JSON are now treated as "field not set" rather than "field set to zero." This matches the existing behavior of `kubectl apply` and the semantic intent of null in JSON.

#### Does this PR introduce a user-facing change?

```release-note
Fixed an inconsistency where kubectl create would convert null resource values (e.g., cpu: null) to zero instead of treating them as unset, causing validation errors that did not occur with kubectl apply.
```

#### Additional documentation e.g., KEPs (Kubernetes Enhancements Proposals), usage docs, etc.

N/A

#### What type of PR is this?
/kind cleanup

#### What this PR does / why we need it:

#### What type of PR is this?

/kind bug

#### What this PR does / why we need it:

If you put a %YAML 1.1 directive at the top of your YAML config file (which is valid per the YAML spec), kubectl apply chokes on it. The YAML stream splitter treats --- as a document boundary no matter what, so it splits the directive into its own "document" and the actual content into another.

The fix makes the splitter recognize when the buffer only has directives (%YAML, %TAG) and keeps the following --- with them instead of splitting there.

Tested on a kind cluster with both single and multi document YAML files using %YAML 1.1 directive. Before the fix kubectl apply errors out, after the fix it applies cleanly.

title: kubelet: prevent scheduling duplicate container starts in computePodActions

labels.name: kind/bug
labels.description: Categorizes issue or PR as related to a bug.

labels.name: sig/node

labels.description: Indicates that a PR should not merge because it has an invalid commit message.

labels.name: needs-triage
labels.description: Indicates an issue or PR lacks a `triage/foo` label and requires one.

body:
/sig node
/kind bug

## What this PR does / why we need it

Fixes #139670

When a CRI `StartContainer` call times out in kubelet but succeeds in the
container runtime (e.g. containerd), kubelet may schedule another container
start on the next `syncPod` cycle. This results in two containers running
simultaneously for the same pod.

**Root cause:**

`computePodActions` uses `FindContainerStatusByName` to check if a container
is running. However, `FindContainerStatusByName` returns only the **first**
matching status. If the first status is `Exited` (kubelet's stale view after
the timeout), it misses a second `Running` status (the container containerd
actually started successfully).

This is already acknowledged in the codebase at line 1393:
// podStatus.FindContainerStatusByName cannot be used because there can be
// multiple container statuses per container

// When there are multiple containers' statuses with the same name,
// the first match will be returned.

**The race condition:**

kubelet calls CreateContainer --> succeeds ✅
kubelet calls StartContainer --> times out ❌ (from kubelet's perspective)
containerd actually started the container successfully ✅
Next syncPod cycle: FindContainerStatusByName returns Exited status
computePodActions appends to ContainersToStart --> second container created 💥
Both containers now Running for the same pod


## How this fixes it

Before appending to `ContainersToStart`, we now scan all entries in
`podStatus.ContainerStatuses` for a running instance with the same container
name. If one is found, we treat it as healthy (`keepCount++`) and skip
scheduling a new start.

This is the same pattern already used in `getContainersToReset()` which
explicitly avoids `FindContainerStatusByName` for the same reason.

## Related

This PR takes a **preventive** approach in `computePodActions` (stops
duplicate from being scheduled). PR #139702 by the original reporter takes
a **reactive** approach in the GC layer (cleans up duplicates after they
exist). Both approaches together provide more complete coverage.

## Testing

- Added unit test case in `TestComputePodActions`:
  `"do not start container when running duplicate exists (issue 139670)"`
  
  The test simulates the race condition by injecting a duplicate Running
  status after an Exited status for the same container name, and verifies
  that `computePodActions` does NOT add the container to `ContainersToStart`.

- All existing `TestComputePodActions` tests pass.

## Which issue(s) this PR fixes
Fixes #139670

**What this PR does / why we need it:**

Fixes an infinite loop in `updateStorageVersionFor` when `singleUpdate` returns `AlreadyExists` or `Conflict` errors. The retry counter is not incremented before `continue`, so the loop never terminates. This can hang API server startup when a StorageVersion object has a persistent conflict during concurrent multi-instance startup.

Also adds a `klog.Infof` log line in the retry branch so transient conflict retries are visible in server logs, matching the existing log format in the general error branch.

Continuation of #138984 which was accidentally closed and could not be reopened (branch history issue). The fix adds `retry++` and a `klog.Infof` line in the `AlreadyExists`/`Conflict` branch, matching the identical pattern in the general error branch below. The bug was introduced in #92459 (2020-02-24).

#### What this PR does / why we need it:

When a multi-document YAML file contains a syntax error in any document after the first, the reported `yaml: line N:` number is relative to the start of that document, not the file, so users are pointed at the wrong line. This change makes `YAMLReader` track the line offset of each document as it splits the stream, threads that offset through `YAMLToJSONDecoder` into `YAMLSyntaxError`, and rewrites the `yaml: line N:` portion of the error message to the file-global line number. Errors without a recognizable line reference are passed through unchanged.

#### Which issue(s) this PR is related to:

Fixes #104607

#### Special notes for your reviewer:
This PR was written in part with the assistance of generative AI; all changes were authored and reviewed by me.

The fix is contained in `staging/src/k8s.io/apimachinery/pkg/util/yaml` (the decoder used by kubectl and other consumers). Single-document files keep their existing messages (offset zero short-circuits).

AI disclosure: this change was prepared with assistance from an AI tool (Claude Code). The diff and tests were reviewed and verified by the submitter.

#### Does this PR introduce a user-facing change?

```release-note
YAML syntax errors in multi-document manifests now report line numbers relative to the file instead of the failing document.
```

title: [InPlacePodVerticalScaling] Allow `BestEffort` pods to specify resources, `Burstable` Pods to set requests==limits

labels.name: sig/node
labels.description: Categorizes an issue or PR as relevant to SIG Node.

labels.name: kind/feature
labels.description: Categorizes issue or PR as related to a new feature.

labels.name: needs-triage
labels.description: Indicates an issue or PR lacks a `triage/foo` label and requires one.

body:
Today, `InPlacePodVerticalScaling` does not allow changes to QoS class. 

This means that a BestEffort pod cannot be updated to add requests or limits, a Burstable pod cannot be updated to set requests == limits.

This limitation is not ideal and leads to strange workarounds, for example having to set requests to be limits-1, just to satisfy the restriction. Additionally, if VPA with `InPlaceOrRecreate` mode happens to make a recommendation for a Burstable pod that would make requests == limits, the patch fails and VPA falls back to evicting the pod. 

I'm opening this issue to revisit the idea of Mutable QoS shape, which was first proposed [as a future enhancement on the IPPVS KEP](https://github.com/kubernetes/enhancements/tree/master/keps/sig-node/1287-in-place-update-pod-resources#mutable-qos-class-shape). 

The proposal is:
- allow BestEffort pods to be freely resized and allow Burstable pods to set requests == limits; however the QoS class of the pod is defined at create time and does not change.
- Guaranteed pods are _not_ allowed to change QoS shape, and must maintain requests == limits
- Burstable pods are _not_ allowed to have all resources removed, and must maintain at least one container with a request or limit set

There is broader discussion around allowing users to explicitly set a QoS class which I am in favor of, but this proposal does not necessarily depend on it.

/kind feature
/sig node

body:
Fixes #131982


/kind bug



Docker credential `auth` fields are base64-decoded and then converted directly to a Go string. Since Go strings can contain arbitrary byte sequences, non-UTF-8 credentials are currently accepted without validation.

This change adds an explicit UTF-8 validation step after base64 decoding in `decodeDockerConfigFieldAuth`. Invalid UTF-8 sequences now return an error instead of being silently accepted.

The implementation follows the existing Kubernetes pattern using `utf8.Valid(...)`, as seen in other validation code paths.

Additionally, regression tests have been added for:

* `TestDockerConfigEntryJSONDecode`
* `TestDecodeDockerConfigFieldAuth`

to ensure non-UTF-8 credentials are rejected through both the JSON deserialization path and the direct helper function.

#### issue(s) this PR is related to:

Fixes #131982

#### Special notes for reviewer:

The change is intentionally minimal and only affects validation of invalid credential data. Valid Docker credentials are unaffected.

#### What type of PR is this?
/kind bug
/sig-windows
/sig-node

<!--
Add one of the following kinds:
/kind bug

/kind documentation
/kind feature

Optionally add one or more of the following kinds if applicable:
/kind api-change
/kind deprecation
/kind failing-test
/kind flake
/kind regression
-->

#### What this PR does / why we need it:
On Windows, when both the CPU Manager (`static` policy) and the Memory Manager
are active under the `WindowsCPUAndMemoryAffinity` feature gate,
`computeFinalCpuSet()` in `internal_container_lifecycle_windows.go` merged the two
managers' results. In the partial-overlap case ("Case 3" — the CPU Manager's CPUs
fall partly outside the Memory Manager's selected NUMA node) it returned the
**union** of the CPU Manager's CPUs and *all* of the NUMA node's CPUs:
That union had two problems:

Isolation violation. The unioned NUMA-node CPUs can include CPUs the CPU Manager exclusively allocated to other containers. Those containers are pinned to the same CPUs, so the affinity masks overlap and the exclusivity guarantee is broken.
Reverted by reconciliation. The expanded set is written only into the CRI request, never into CPU Manager state. The CPU Manager's reconcileState() loop reads state as the source of truth and re-applies the original (smaller) set via UpdateContainerResources, so the union is a no-op in steady state.
This PR makes computeFinalCpuSet() always return the CPU Manager's allocated set
when the CPU Manager has assigned CPUs (collapsing the former Cases 1/2/3). The CPU
Manager's allocation is authoritative — it already accounts for Topology Manager
hints. NUMA-node CPUs are used only when the Memory Manager is the sole provider.


#### Which issue(s) this PR is related to:
<!--
Please link relevant issues to help with tracking.

To automatically close the linked issue(s) when this PR is merged,
add the word "Fixes" before the issue number or link.
Do not use "Fixes" if the PR is of kind `failing-test` or `flake`.

<issue link> (issue in a different repository)
KEP: https://github.com/kubernetes/enhancements/issues/<kep-issue-number>

If there is no associated issue, then write "N/A".
-->

#### Special notes for your reviewer:

#### Does this PR introduce a user-facing change?
On Windows nodes with the alpha `WindowsCPUAndMemoryAffinity` feature gate enabled, a guaranteed container's CPU affinity now always matches the CPU Manager's exclusive allocation. Previously, when the CPU Manager and Memory Manager selected partially different NUMA nodes, the affinity could be widened to the union of both sets, which could include CPUs exclusively allocated to other containers and break CPU isolation.

title: Reduce verbosity of conntrack test case

labels.name: sig/network
labels.description: Categorizes an issue or PR as relevant to SIG Network.

labels.name: triage/accepted
labels.description: Indicates an issue or PR is ready to be actively worked on.

body: The following test case emits 6000 lines of the repeating pattern in the normal case: ``` (date) Try: (number) ``` https://github.com/kubernetes/kubernetes/blob/756939600b9a7180fc2df6550a4585b638875e67/test/e2e/network/conntrack.go#L409 This causes significant load on the logging outputter with 500+ lines emitted per second. Notably, when the testsuite is run from GitHub actions, and the test runner emits too many lines of output, this causes the GitHub actions UI to stall and potentially fail to render test results. This makes for a frustrating contributor experience, because the upstream Kubernetes tests often fail in ways that do not demonstrate the failure. Example: https://github.com/cilium/cilium/actions/runs/27361735725/job/80851060186#step:18:1 ~Presumably the test output is looking to identify when a "hang" or failed connection occurs and pinpointing the timestamp of that occurrance. Rather than emitting a constant stream of output for the normal case, perhaps this can be adjusted to ensure the command times out within a reasonable period, then emits a meaningful log on error.~ EDIT: Given the command is running via UDP, there is no connection and presumably therefore no timeout. Is the intention to understand the distribution of timestamps for the 3K sent packets in retrospect? Or could we just drop the echo logs entirely?

body:
/kind cleanup
                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                   
  Migrates all `metav1.Condition` field validations to Declarative Validation as part of KEP-5073.                                                                                                                                                                 
                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                   
  This PR:
  - Adds declarative validation tags for all Condition fields:                                                                                                                                                                                                     
    - `Reason`: `+k8s:required`, `+k8s:maxLength=1024`                                                                                                                                                                                                             
    - `Message`: `+k8s:optional`, `+k8s:maxLength=32768`                                                                                                                                                                                                           
    - `ConditionStatus` type: `+k8s:enum`                                                                                                                                                                                                                          
    - `Status` field: `+k8s:required`                                                                                                                                                                                                                              
    - `Type`: `+k8s:format=k8s-label-key`
    - `ObservedGeneration`: `+k8s:optional`, `+k8s:minimum=0`                                                                                                                                                                                                      
  - Marks corresponding handwritten validations as covered by declarative validation.
  - Splits the `Status` handwritten check to produce `Required` for empty values                                                                                                                                                                                   
    and `NotSupported` for invalid non-empty values, aligning with declarative behavior.                                                                                                                                                                           
  - Adds declarative validation test coverage for all migrated fields.                                                                                                                                                                                             
  - Regenerates validation code and generated declarative validation tests.                                                                                                                                                                                        
                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                   
  Note: The `Reason` format validation (regex pattern check) is not migrated as                                                                                                                                                                                    
  declarative regex pattern validation is not yet supported.                                                                                                                                                                                                       
                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                   
  #### Which issue(s) this PR is related to:
  Part of #139638                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                  
  KEP: https://github.com/kubernetes/enhancements/tree/master/keps/sig-api-machinery/5073-declarative-validation                                                                                                                                                   
                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                   
  #### Special notes for your reviewer:                                                                                                                                                                                                                            
  This PR covers all 5 batches from the metav1.Condition migration tracking issue:                                                                                                                                                                                 
  - Batch 1: `Reason` (`+k8s:required`, `+k8s:maxLength=1024`)                                                                                                                                                                                                     
  - Batch 2: `Message` (`+k8s:optional`, `+k8s:maxLength=32768`)                                                                                                                                                                                                   
  - Batch 3: `ConditionStatus` type (`+k8s:enum`) + `Status` field (`+k8s:required`)                                                                                                                                                                               
  - Batch 4: `Type` (`+k8s:format=k8s-label-key`)                                                                                                                                                                                                                  
  - Batch 5: `ObservedGeneration` (`+k8s:optional`, `+k8s:minimum=0`)
                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                   
  Each batch is structured as one commit per DV tag per the recommended PR structure in the tracking issue.                                                                                                                                                        
                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                   
  #### Does this PR introduce a user-facing change?                                                                                                                                                                                                                
  release-note 
  NONE

body:
This flips `ConcurrentWatchObjectDecode` on by default (Beta, 1.37) to exercise CI with the gate enabled. PR is not intended to merge, but collect data around benchmarks.

### Unit benchmarks

Watch cache initialization for pods ([`BenchmarkCacherInit`](https://github.com/kubernetes/kubernetes/blob/42d9ed9c47706cb04457410e387e07889203b888/staging/src/k8s.io/apiserver/pkg/storage/cacher/cacher_init_bench_test.go#L41)) is roughly 45% faster, 7.3s down to 4.0s populating 150k pods

https://github.com/kubernetes/kubernetes/pull/139606 points to benchmarking optimal # of goroutines as well as cpu tradeoff of selected number (10).

```
pkg: k8s.io/apiserver/pkg/storage/cacher
              │  gate=off   │            gate=on             │
              │   sec/op    │   sec/op     vs base           │
CacherInit      7.290 ± 43%   3.996 ± 44%  -45.18% (p=0.000 n=20)

              │  gate=off   │           gate=on            │
              │ pods/cache  │ pods/cache   vs base         │
CacherInit      150.0k ± 0%   150.0k ± 0%  ~ (p=1.000 n=20)

              │  gate=off    │           gate=on            │
              │     B/op     │     B/op      vs base        │
CacherInit      11.01Gi ± 8%   11.13Gi ± 6%  ~ (p=0.265 n=20)

              │  gate=off   │            gate=on            │
              │  allocs/op  │  allocs/op   vs base          │
CacherInit      64.61M ± 0%   65.35M ± 0%  +1.14% (p=0.000 n=20)
```

### Scale Test (5k nodes)
- [gate ON (#139679)](https://prow.k8s.io/view/gs/kubernetes-ci-logs/pr-logs/pull/139679/pull-kubernetes-gce-master-scale-performance-5000/2065468764312309760)
- [gate OFF (#139476)](https://prow.k8s.io/view/gs/kubernetes-ci-logs/pr-logs/pull/139476/pull-kubernetes-gce-master-scale-performance-5000/2062929683812454400)

Ran the 5k presubmit with gate on/off.

Watch cache is reinitiailzed in the middle with roughly 165k (exemplar) pods. (3 apiservers, one is restarted at peak)

| watch-cache init (restarted apiserver) | gate off | gate on |
| --- | --- | --- |
| pods | 13.4s | 8.3s (-38%) |
| deployments | 1.27s | 0.82s (-35%) |
| replicasets | 1.26s | 0.86s (-32%) |

### Decode overhead

Addressing the goroutine-multiplier question from https://github.com/kubernetes/kubernetes/pull/126329#discussion_r1691415735.

The 1-to-12 goroutine multiplier from the original review (`processEventConcurrency=10` decoders plus 2 coordinators) is per etcd watch, and there is one watch per resource type. At ~500 resources (large cluster with many CRDs), that caps out around 6000 goroutines, compared to ~200k total goroutines from the apiserver. And it only reaches that cap on a relist. In steady state events trickle in one at a time, so the fan-out never engages and the transient decoders drain away. What remains is the 2 persistent coordinators per watch, versus 1 on the serial path, so +1 goroutine per resource (65 in this run). Basically invisible in steady state. 

The differences in these numbers is insignificant and is likely just noise.

| metric (5k) | gate off | gate on |
| --- | --- | --- |
| peak goroutines (loaded apiserver) | ~207-211k | ~207-212k |
| OS threads | 126 | 122 |
| total apiserver CPU (cpu-seconds) | 64,417 | 64,807 |
| apiserver RSS after relist | 16.2G | 14.4G |

#### goroutines after restart apiserver 

There's an overhead with the gate on with the 2 extra coordinators/watch per resource.

| min since restart | 0 | 5 | 10 | 15 | 20 | 25 | 30 | 40 | 50 |
| --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |
| gate OFF | 2,275 | 2,313 | 2,289 | 2,317 | 2,306 | 2,320 | 2,317 | 2,325 | 2,713 |
| gate ON | 2,343 | 2,390 | 2,359 | 2,387 | 2,366 | 2,416 | 2,428 | 2,628 | 3,022 |

title: [DRA] Cohort borrowing bypasses quota when ClusterQueues use different unit scales for the same resource

labels.name: kind/bug
labels.description: Categorizes issue or PR as related to a bug.

labels.name: needs-sig
labels.description: Indicates an issue or PR lacks a `sig/foo` label and requires one.

labels.name: needs-triage
labels.description: Indicates an issue or PR lacks a `triage/foo` label and requires one.

body:
### What happened?

  When two ClusterQueues share a cohort and use different quota units for the same counter-based resource (e.g., CQ1 with `nominalQuota: "20096Mi"` and CQ2 with `nominalQuota: "1"`), the CQ with device-count-style quota borrows unlimited capacity from the counter-based CQ. Kueue interprets `"1"` as 1 byte and allows borrowing of billions of bytes from the shared pool.

This seems to be a cohort validation gap — Kueue has no cross-CQ validation that quota units are compatible for shared resources. Partitionable Devices surfaces this because it introduces counter-based charging (memory quantities) alongside the traditional device-count style, making mixed-unit cohorts a misconfiguration.

### What did you expect to happen?

Kueue should validate or warn when ClusterQueues in the same cohort have quota values in incompatible unit scales for the same resource. A CQ with `nominalQuota: "1"` alongside a CQ with `nominalQuota: "20096Mi"` for the same counter-based resource is almost certainly a misconfiguration.

### How can we reproduce it (as minimally and precisely as possible)?

1. Create two ClusterQueues in the same cohort with `gpu.memory` as a covered resource:
     - CQ1: `nominalQuota: "20096Mi"` (counter-based, sized for one 3g.20gb MIG profile)
     - CQ2: `nominalQuota: "1"` (device-count style)
     - Both with `borrowingLimit` unset and `reclaimWithinCohort: Any`

2. Submit 4 workloads to CQ2's LocalQueue, each requesting a `1g.5gb` MIG partition via DRA ResourceClaimTemplate

3. Check CQ2 usage:
  $ kubectl get clusterqueue cq2-device-count -o json | jq '.status.flavorsUsage[].resources[]'
```
  {
    "borrowed": "20401094655",
    "name": "gpu.memory",
    "total": "20401094656"
  }
```
  CQ2 borrowed ~19,456Mi from a 1-byte quota — 20 billion times over.

4. Check CQ1 usage:
 ```
 $ kubectl get clusterqueue cq1-counter -o json | jq '{
    admittedWorkloads: .status.admittedWorkloads,
    usage: .status.flavorsUsage[].resources[]
  }'
  {
    "admittedWorkloads": 0,
    "usage": {
      "borrowed": "0",
      "name": "gpu.memory",
      "total": "0"
    }
  }
```
CQ1 shows 0 usage while its pool is almost entirely consumed by CQ2.

5. Submit a workload to CQ1. CQ1 preempts one CQ2 workload via `reclaimWithinCohort`, but CQ2 still retains 3 workloads borrowing ~14,592Mi. The preempted workload is requeued and re-borrows immediately.

### Anything else we need to know?

**Some points to highlight:**

- No validation: Kueue accepts the mixed-unit cohort without warning
- No borrowing limit: CQ2 borrows 20 billion times its own quota
- Preempted CQ2 workloads are requeued and re-borrow immediately, creating an endless preemption cycle
- No cross-CQ visibility: CQ1 shows 0 usage while its capacity is consumed by CQ2

<details>
Kubernetes version: v1.35.5
</details>

body:
#### What type of PR is this?

/kind feature
/sig scheduling

#### What this PR does / why we need it:

This PR introduces a new scheduler framework extension point: `PodGroupPostFilter`. 
This extension point runs after `PostFilter` but operates on PodGroups (used in GangScheduling) rather than individual pods.

Previously, `WorkloadAwarePreemption` logic relied on a hack inside `NewFramework` where the `DefaultPreemption` plugin was copied directly from `pluginsMap` into `podGroupPostFilterPlugins` array if the `WorkloadAwarePreemption` feature gate was enabled. This PR removes that hard-coded hack and introduces a fully configurable `PodGroupPostFilter` extension point.

Changes in this PR:
1. Adds `PodGroupPostFilter` to the framework and validation API types, exporting the interfaces via `staging/src/k8s.io/kube-scheduler/framework/interface.go` so out-of-tree plugins can implement it.
2. Implements `RunPodGroupPostFilterPlugins` inside `framework.go` to properly execute registered plugins.
3. Removes the hardcoded preemption logic in `NewFramework`.
4. Adds feature gate validation in `validation.go` and `default_plugins.go` so `PodGroupPostFilter` plugins are properly merged and validated depending on the `WorkloadAwarePreemption` feature gate.
5. Adds a `KEP_UPDATES.md` document highlighting minor proposed updates/clarifications to the Workload Aware Preemption KEP regarding the extension point interface definition.

#### Which issue(s) this PR is related to:

KEP: https://github.com/kubernetes/enhancements/tree/master/keps/sig-scheduling/5710-workload-aware-preemption

#### Special notes for your reviewer:

- This PR is split into logical commits mimicking previous extension point PRs:
  1. Documentation (`KEP_UPDATES.md` detailing API interface changes)
  2. API and codegen (`make update`)
  3. Framework iteration implementation and `TestPodGroupPostFilterIteration` integration test
  4. Validation and default plugin configuration
- I have removed a test case in `schedule_one_podgroup_test.go` (`disables pod group post filter when DefaultPreemption is not registered`) because `PodGroupPostFilter` is now a generic extension point that any valid plugin can use, making the hard-coded restriction obsolete.

#### Does this PR introduce a user-facing change?

```release-note
Introduces `PodGroupPostFilter` extension point to the scheduling framework. This replaces internal hardcoding for `WorkloadAwarePreemption` with a proper, configurable extension point for operating on PodGroups.
```

#### Additional documentation e.g., KEPs (Kubernetes Enhancement Proposals), usage docs, etc.:

```docs
- [KEP]: https://github.com/kubernetes/enhancements/tree/master/keps/sig-scheduling/5710-workload-aware-preemption
```

title: kubelet creates duplicate running containers

labels.name: kind/bug
labels.description: Categorizes issue or PR as related to a bug.

labels.name: sig/node
labels.description: Categorizes an issue or PR as relevant to SIG Node.

labels.name: needs-triage
labels.description: Indicates an issue or PR lacks a `triage/foo` label and requires one.

body:
### What happened?

In a stress test cluster, we discovered that a Pod's container was created twice and was simultaneously in the Running state.
`[root@master1 home]# kubectl get -n network-system -o wide pod`
`
NAME                                 READY  STATUS   RESTARTS  AGE    IP            NODE       NOMINATED NODE   READINESS GATES
`
`
common-test-deploy-54c5d667c4-zbnbc  1/1    Running  1         3h28m  10.88.189.72  127.0.0.1  <none>           <none>
`
`[root@master1 home]# crictl ps`
`
CONTAINER      IMAGE          CREATED      STATE    NAME                 ATTEMPT  POD ID         POD
`
`
73eca2411f5f7  84eebb9ca1734  3 hours ago  Running  common-test-support  1        a9c301a1bb1b9  common-test-deploy-54c5d667c4-zbnbc
`
`
65ed387ac6cc1  84eebb9ca1734  3 hours ago  Running  common-test-support  0        a9c301a1bb1b9  common-test-deploy-54c5d667c4-zbnbc
`

Unfortunately, we did not retain the complete logs, and after some attempts, we were unable to manually reproduce the issue. 
Through a step-by-step analysis of the code, we found that the problem might be caused by kubelet repeatedly calling the CRI interface to create and start the container. The first interface call might have timed out, but containerd had actually already started the container successfully. Then, kubelet issued a second interface call, which started a new container.

I also found a previous issue [#122585](https://github.com/kubernetes/kubernetes/issues/122585)

### What did you expect to happen?

I expect there should only be one container in the Running state, and only the most recently started container should be retained.

### How can we reproduce it (as minimally and precisely as possible)?

This is indeed a rather extreme scenario, and we have only observed it once in recent years. I tried many methods but couldn't reproduce it. It only occurs when the code in kubelet is modified to repeatedly restart the container, but this is not a very appropriate method.

### Anything else we need to know?

body:
When a node's Ready condition transitions from `false` to `unknown` (for example, when containerd crashes and then the kubelet also loses connectivity), the node controller was not calling `MarkPodsNotReady`. Pods kept their Ready condition set to `true`, so traffic could still be sent to the broken node.

The bug is in `monitorNodeHealth`. The transition checks that trigger `MarkPodsNotReady` and retry scheduling only checked for transitions starting from `Ready=true`. This completely misses the `false -> unknown` transition.

The fix introduces a `transitionedToNotReady` predicate that covers both `true -> false/unknown` and `false -> unknown` transitions, and refactors the switch-fallthrough block into clean conditional statements.

Fixes #112733

body:
#### What type of PR is this?

/kind bug

#### What this PR does / why we need it:

During `kubeadm join`, the mandatory `kubeadm-config` ConfigMap fetch
uses `GetConfigMapWithShortRetry`, which has a 350ms polling budget.
The GET call uses `context.Background()` instead of the polling context,
so when the API server is slow to respond, a single attempt blocks for
up to 10 seconds (the client timeout from `ToClientSet`), exhausting
the polling budget with no retry. Since this call site has no fallback,
the join fails.

This PR adds a `shortConfigMapGet` parameter to
`FetchInitConfigurationFromCluster` and `getInitConfigurationFromCluster`:

- When `false` (used by `kubeadm join`, `kubeadm upgrade`, `kubeadm certs`):
  the `kubeadm-config` ConfigMap is fetched using `KubernetesAPICallTimeout`
  (default 1 minute, user-configurable) with retries. This is consistent
  with how `getAPIEndpointFromPodAnnotation` handles API calls in the same
  file, and gives the mandatory fetch a longer retry window.

- When `true` (used by `kubeadm reset`): the existing
  `GetConfigMapWithShortRetry` is used, preserving the fast fallback
  behavior for callers that don't need a long retry.

`GetConfigMapWithShortRetry` itself is not modified.

#### Which issue(s) this PR is related to:

Related to https://github.com/kubernetes/kubeadm/issues/3294

#### Special notes for your reviewer:

The issue was observed in Cluster API e2e tests where `kubeadm join`
for a worker node would fail because the API server was temporarily
slow to respond through the control plane endpoint. The discovery
phase (cluster-info from kube-public) would succeed after retries,
but the immediately following ConfigMap fetch would make a single
attempt and fail.

A standalone reproduction test is available at:
https://gist.github.com/damdo/14f02f7398de1797ee0a8025334aa4de

Related:
- https://github.com/kubernetes/kubeadm/issues/3294
- https://github.com/kubernetes/kubernetes/pull/138449

#### Does this PR introduce a user-facing change?

```release-note
kubeadm: during "kubeadm join", use the KubernetesAPICall timeout
(default 1 minute) when fetching the kubeadm-config ConfigMap, instead
of the short 350ms retry used for optional component configs. A new
shortConfigMapGet parameter is added to FetchInitConfigurationFromCluster
so that callers like "kubeadm reset" can still use the short retry.
```

#### What type of PR is this?

/kind bug

#### What this PR does / why we need it:

The apiserver endpoint reconcilers only repaired drifted endpoint port attributes when `reconcilePorts` was true. The periodic call site (`UpdateKubernetesService(false)`) leaves that flag false, so manual edits to the `kubernetes` service endpoint's port number, port name, or protocol were never corrected, while IP drift was repaired as expected.

This PR extends the port comparison in `checkEndpointSubsetFormat` (instance-count reconciler) and `checkEndpointSubsetFormatWithLease` (lease reconciler) so the single configured port is reconciled on the periodic path as well, preserving the design intent of the `reconcilePorts` parameter. Unit tests cover drifted port number, drifted port name, drifted protocol, and the no-drift no-op case for both reconcilers.

#### Which issue(s) this PR is related to:

Fixes #104252

#### Special notes for your reviewer:
This PR was written in part with the assistance of generative AI; all changes were authored and reviewed by me.

The fix follows the direction aojea outlined in the issue thread: extend the existing reconciliation comparison rather than adding a separate reset path. The lease reconciler shares the identical gap, so both reconcilers receive the same one-comparison change.

AI disclosure: this change was prepared with assistance from an AI tool (Claude Code). The diff and tests were reviewed and verified by the submitter.

#### Does this PR introduce a user-facing change?

```release-note
Fixed the default kubernetes service Endpoints reconcilers to also repair drifted port name, port number, and protocol. Previously only IP drift was reconciled.
```

body:
#### What type of PR is this?

/kind bug

#### What this PR does / why we need it:

During graceful node shutdown, `Node.status.volumesInUse` was not properly cleared before kubelet exited. This caused volumes to remain "stuck" in the attach/detach controller's cache until the 6-minute force-detach timeout, preventing volumes from being attached to other nodes.

**Root cause:** Multiple race conditions in the shutdown flow:

1. **WaitForAllPodsUnmount only waited for NodeUnpublishVolume**, not NodeUnstageVolume. The full detach chain (Populator → Unpublish → Unstage → MarkDetached) happens asynchronously with no synchronization.

2. **The only syncNodeStatus call was fire-and-forget** (`go m.syncNodeStatus`), called before volume cleanup, and not guaranteed to complete before kubelet exits.

3. **The systemd inhibit lock was released without any synchronous status update**, allowing systemd to kill kubelet before the cleared state was persisted.

**Fix:**

1. **Extended `WaitForAllPodsUnmount`** to wait for `GetUnmountedVolumes() == 0`, ensuring the entire detach chain completes before returning. This also works when called with an empty pod list (handles orphan volumes from pods deleted/evicted just before shutdown).

2. **Added initial `WaitForAllPodsUnmount(nil)` call** in `killPods()` before the group loop to handle pre-existing orphan volumes that are still being unstaged.

3. **Added synchronous `syncNodeStatus` call** in the defer block of `processShutdownEvent`, ensuring it runs after `killPods` completes and before the inhibit lock is released. This guarantees the cleared `volumesInUse` state is persisted to the API server before systemd can terminate kubelet. The existing fire-and-forget async call is kept to quickly mark the node as NotReady for the scheduler.

#### Which issue(s) this PR is related to:

KEP: https://github.com/kubernetes/enhancements/issues/2000

Related to PR #136029 (AD controller changes) — this fix handles the graceful shutdown case (kubelet can help), while #136029 handles the node deletion case (kubelet can't help).

#### Special notes for your reviewer:

This kubelet-side fix is complementary to the AD controller 6-minute force-detach mechanism. With this fix, volumes are available for re-attachment immediately after shutdown, rather than waiting for the 6-minute timeout.

#### Does this PR introduce a user-facing change?

```release-note
During graceful node shutdown, volumesInUse is now properly cleared from Node.status before kubelet exits. Volumes can be immediately attached to other nodes after the node shuts down, without waiting for the 6-minute force-detach timeout.
```

#### Additional documentation e.g., KEPs (Kubernetes Enhancement Proposals), usage docs, etc.:

```docs

```

---

## Manual E2E test: Verifying volumesInUse is cleared during shutdown

### Test setup

- Kubelet config: GracefulNodeShutdown + GracefulNodeShutdownBasedOnPodPriority enabled
- Test pod: `default/test-disk-pod` with a PVC backed by a CSI block storage volume
- Shutdown priority groups:
  ```yaml
  shutdownGracePeriodByPodPriority:
    - priority: 0              → 20s
    - priority: 2000000000     → 15s  (cluster-critical)
    - priority: 2000001000     → 30s  (node-critical: csi-plugin, cni, node-local-dns)
  ```

### Result: volumesInUse cleared in 1.53 seconds

The API server audit log captures the exact moment kubelet patches `volumesInUse: null`:

**API server audit log** (node status patches):
```
# Normal heartbeat before shutdown
requestReceivedTimestamp: 2026-06-11T17:56:18.273448Z
userAgent: kubelet
requestObject: {"status":{"conditions":[{"lastHeartbeatTime":"2026-06-11T17:56:18Z","type":"Ready"}]}}

# 1st syncNodeStatus (async): marks node as NotReady
requestReceivedTimestamp: 2026-06-11T17:57:05.678343Z
userAgent: kubelet
requestObject: {"status":{"conditions":[{"lastTransitionTime":"2026-06-11T17:57:05Z","message":"node is shutting down","reason":"KubeletNotReady","status":"False","type":"Ready"}]}}

# 2nd syncNodeStatus (sync, in defer): clears volumesInUse ← OUR FIX
requestReceivedTimestamp: 2026-06-11T17:57:07.198629Z
userAgent: kubelet
requestObject: {"status":{"volumesInUse":null, ...}}

# KCM ADC reacts in 106ms: clears volumesAttached
requestReceivedTimestamp: 2026-06-11T17:57:07.304106Z
userAgent: kube-controller-manager/...attachdetach-controller
requestObject: {"status":{"volumesAttached":null}}
```

**Kubelet logs** (from `journalctl -b -1 -u kubelet`, timezone +8:00):
```
01:57:05.675  Shutdown manager detected new shutdown event
01:57:05.676  Node became not ready (reason: KubeletNotReady, message: node is shutting down)
01:57:05.677  Killing pod priority=0: test-disk-pod gracePeriod=10
01:57:06.284  Finished killing pod: test-disk-pod
01:57:06.397  UnmountVolume started for CSI volume (NodeUnpublishVolume)
01:57:06.400  UnmountVolume.TearDown succeeded for CSI volume
01:57:06.498  UnmountDevice started for CSI volume (NodeUnstageVolume)
01:57:06.501  UnmountDevice succeeded for CSI volume
01:57:06.685  Done waiting for all pods in group to terminate, gracePeriod=20 priority=0
01:57:06.685  Killing pod priority=2000001000: csi-plugin, terway, node-local-dns, kube-proxy
01:57:06.893  All node-critical pods killed (208ms)
01:57:07.16x  Done waiting for all pods in group to terminate, gracePeriod=30 priority=2000001000
01:57:07.198  [syncNodeStatus in defer] patches volumesInUse: null ← OUR FIX
01:57:07.209  Shutdown manager completed, inhibit lock released
01:57:23.963  Kubelet: Ready=True (new boot)
```

The KCM attach/detach controller saw `volumesInUse: null` and immediately set `volumesAttached: null`, making the volume available for other nodes **without waiting for the 6-minute timeout**.

body:
**What type of PR is this?**

/kind feature
/sig node

**What this PR does / why we need it:**

The `"Generic (PLEG): container finished"` log message is the primary signal for container kill events (e.g. `exitCode=137` for SIGKILL), but it only included the pod UID and container ID:

    generic.go:410] "Generic (PLEG): container finished" podID="0137706b-..." containerID="79923472..." exitCode=137

Attributing such an event to a workload required a separate lookup from pod UID to pod name, which makes log-based metrics and alerting on SIGKILL/OOM events unnecessarily difficult.

This PR adds the pod namespace/name and the container name to the message:

    generic.go:412] "Generic (PLEG): container finished" pod="my-namespace/my-pod" podUID="0137706b-..." containerName="my-container" containerID="79923472..." exitCode=137

Notes:
- The container statuses were already fetched during relisting; the lookup map
  now caches the full status instead of just the exit code, so there is no
  additional runtime cost.
- The message string and the `exitCode` key are unchanged, so existing
  log filters keep working.
- The `podID` key was renamed to `podUID` to match the structured logging
  conventions used elsewhere in the kubelet.

**Which issue(s) this PR fixes:**

Fixes #139496

Related: #125785, #96725

**Special notes for your reviewer:**

The kubelet never sends SIGKILL itself (the runtime escalates after the grace period, or the kernel OOM killer acts directly), so the issue's secondary request for an explicit "SIGKILL sent" log is intentionally not implemented.

The kubelet-initiated kill path already logs "Killing container with a grace period" with full workload identity; this PR closes the gap on the observation side.

**Does this PR introduce a user-facing change?**


```release-note
kubelet: the "Generic (PLEG): container finished" log message now includes the pod namespace/name and the container name. The `podID` key was renamed to `podUID`.
```

#### What type of PR is this?
/kind cleanup

#### What this PR does / why we need it:
This is a partial fix for #131893.

It reduces one remaining non-agnhost image usage in autoscaling e2e by moving the autoscaling resource-consumer workload from the standalone `resource-consumer` image onto a new `agnhost resource-consumer` subcommand.

That keeps the existing autoscaling behavior while moving this test path toward the project's preferred consolidation on `agnhost`.

Windows-specific `resource-consumer` usage in `test/e2e/windows/eviction.go` is intentionally left unchanged in this PR because it depends on `testlimit.exe`.

#### Which issue(s) this PR fixes:
Partial fix for #131893

#### Special notes for your reviewer:
This PR copies the existing standalone resource-consumer behavior into `agnhost` and switches the autoscaling e2e framework to invoke it via `command: ["/agnhost", "resource-consumer"]`.

Focused verification:
- `go test ./test/images/agnhost/resource-consumer`
- `go test ./test/images/agnhost -run ^`

title: scheduler: fix pending pods metric accounting for entities with size > 1

labels.name: kind/bug

labels.description: Indicates a PR lacks a `priority/foo` label and requires one.

labels.name: needs-triage
labels.description: Indicates an issue or PR lacks a `triage/foo` label and requires one.

body:
#### What type of PR is this?

/kind bug

#### What this PR does / why we need it:

`Heap.Delete` decrements the metric recorder by the size of the passed object. But the passed object may be just a lookup object, whose size doesn't match the stored one.

The scheduling queue deletes entities from the activeQ/backoffQ heaps using such lookups. For pod groups (added in #138567), the lookup built by `newQueuedPodGroupInfoForLookup` has no member pods, so its `Size()` is 0, while the stored group has `Size() == N`. Whenever a queued pod group is deleted via a lookup (a new member pod joins a queued group, a member pod is deleted, the group is activated out of the backoffQ), the `scheduler_pending_pods` gauge is decremented by 0 after having been incremented by N, so it stays inflated forever. For example, adding 3 pods of the same group one by one leaves the gauge at 6 instead of 3.

Fixed by decrementing by the size of the removed object instead. Also adjust the metric by the size delta in `AddOrUpdate` when an update replaces the stored object - same kind of problem, though currently a no-op since no caller changes the size on update.

Individual pods always have `Size() == 1`, so they are not affected.

#### Which issue(s) this PR is related to:

N/A
